chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly provides a working link to the MIRADOR specification and source code in Reference [39] (https://gitlab.cs.fau.de/i4/micro-replication/mirador). It also references the BFT-SMaRt repository for comparison. This meets the criteria for full reproducibility.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation section describes experiments conducted on a set of five physical servers (8 cores, 3.60 GHz, 16 GB RAM, Ubuntu 20.04.4). The authors ran their Java-based MIRADOR prototype and BFT-SMaRt library on this hardware to measure latency, CPU usage, and throughput under various workloads (YCSB) and network conditions.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper defines a clear fault model in Section II ('Background and System Model') and Section V. It explicitly states that nodes interact over an 'unreliable, asynchronous network' where transient failures may occur but messages eventually arrive. For the specific protocol MIRADOR, it specifies tolerance for 'up to f Byzantine faults in each cluster', defining precise assumptions about arbitrary behavior of faulty replicas.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides a rigorous data-driven analysis of trade-offs in Section VI-B ('Performance'). It quantifies latency (e.g., response times around 5ms vs <2ms for BFT-SMaRt), CPU usage, and throughput using YCSB benchmarks. It explicitly discusses the overhead introduced by pull-based communication versus push-based accelerators and measures the resource consumption of running micro replicas in isolated JVMs.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.major_weakness: The paper does not include a dedicated section or formal mathematical proof for the safety and liveness properties of the MIRADOR protocol; it relies on TLA+ model checking for selected parts rather than a complete formal verification.
